title: He Pūrākau o te Awa: The Living Stories of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau of Mangakōtukutuku awa (stream) - exploring how our tūpuna used traditional knowledge systems to read weather patterns, monitor water health, and maintain ecological balance through whakataukī, environmental indicators, and lunar cycles specific to our local waterway
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret at least three traditional environmental indicators from Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau that predict weather and seasonal changes around Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Compare traditional Māori knowledge systems with modern scientific approaches to water monitoring through hands-on water quality testing at three specific sites along the awa
Create a digital storymap that documents both traditional knowledge and contemporary data about Mangakōtukutuku Stream, demonstrating understanding of mātauranga Māori principles
Collaborate with local kaumātua to identify one actionable conservation strategy for the awa that blends traditional practices with modern environmental science

activities.title: Whakarongo ki te Awa: Listening to the Water's Stories
activities.description: Students visit Mangakōtukutuku Stream with kaumātua to learn traditional indicators of water health - observing insect life (ngā pūwerewere), water clarity (te mahea o te wai), and plant growth patterns that our tūpuna used to assess ecosystem balance

activities.title: Ngā Tohu o te Rangi: Sky Knowledge Workshop
activities.description: Local knowledge holders teach students to read weather patterns using traditional methods - observing cloud formations specific to Heretaunga, wind patterns from Te Mata Peak, and lunar cycles (maramataka) that guided planting and fishing activities along the awa

activities.title: He Awa Whiria: Braided Knowledge Investigation
activities.description: Students conduct water quality testing using modern scientific tools alongside traditional assessment methods, creating comparative data sets at three key sites: the headwaters, mid-reach, and confluence with Tukituki River

activities.title: Digital Pūrākau: Story Mapping Project
activities.description: Using GIS technology, students create interactive maps that layer traditional place names, historical pūrākau, contemporary environmental data, and oral histories collected from local kaikōrero about Mangakōtukutuku Stream

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga Action Plan
activities.description: Groups develop practical conservation proposals for the awa that integrate traditional knowledge (e.g., planting traditional medicinal plants along banks) with modern science, presenting to a panel including local kaumātua and council representatives

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and knowledge holders from local marae (Pākōwhai Marae)
Digital copies of 'Ngā Pūrākau o Heretaunga' historical manuscripts from Hastings Library Māori collection
Water testing kits and iPads with ArcGIS StoryMaps application
Video interviews with local farmers who practice traditional land management techniques along Mangakōtukutuku Stream

tasks: Comparative analysis report (800 words) examining traditional and scientific approaches to water monitoring
Digital storymap presentation demonstrating layered understanding of cultural and environmental knowledge
Group conservation proposal with practical implementation plan for awa restoration
Reflective journal documenting engagement with kaumātua and personal learning journey

nzcAlignment: Understand how people pass on and sustain culture and heritage for different reasons and that this has consequences for people
Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
